part.text:
# Concurrency Control with Named Queues

## Background

Manage task concurrency using named queues to ensure only a specific number of tasks run simultaneously.

## Requirements

* Initialize a Trigger.dev project in `/home/user/queue-project`.
* Define a named queue `critical-queue-${trial_id}` with a `concurrencyLimit` of 1.
* Create a task `criticalTask-${trial_id}` in `trigger/critical.ts` that uses this queue.
* Use `trial_id` from `/logs/trial_id`.
* The task should simulate work by waiting for 5 seconds.

## Implementation Guide

1. Use the `queue` function to define the queue.
2. Assign the queue to the task in the `task()` definition.

## Constraints

* Project path: /home/user/queue-project
* Use Trigger.dev V4 SDK.

## Integrations

* Trigger.dev

# Concurrency Control with Named Queues

## Background

Manage task concurrency using named queues to ensure only a specific number of tasks run simultaneously.

## Requirements

* Initialize a Trigger.dev project in `/home/user/queue-project`.
* Define a named queue `critical-queue-${trial_id}` with a `concurrencyLimit` of 1.
* Create a task `criticalTask-${trial_id}` in `trigger/critical.ts` that uses this queue.
* Use `trial_id` from `/logs/trial_id`.
* The task should simulate work by waiting for 5 seconds.

## Implementation Guide

1. Use the `queue` function to define the queue.
2. Assign the queue to the task in the `task()` definition.

## Constraints

* Project path: /home/user/queue-project
* Use Trigger.dev V4 SDK.

## Integrations

* Trigger.dev

# Concurrency and queues | Trigger.dev
created: 2026-03-27, last updated: 2026-04-15, [Read more](https://trigger.dev/product/concurrency-and-queues)

# Concurrency and fair queues
Trigger.dev provides flexible concurrency control to manage multiple task runs efficiently.
Configure tasks to run sequentially or in parallel, and use concurrency keys to create custom queue logic for different users or tiers.
...
#### In parallel
### Execute your tasks in parallel
By setting a high concurrency value along with batch triggering, you can execute many tasks in parallel.
This approach is ideal for high-priority tasks with variable spikes.
concurrentLimit: 3
#### Per-tenant queuing
### Configure separate queues per user using concurrency keys
There are many situations where you may want different concurrencies; if certain users are on higher tier plans for example.
With concurrency keys you can create bespoke queue logic for each of your users.
Granular control over your user's concurrency allows you to prioritise your most valued user's tasks.
## Concurrency and queues example code
```
import { task } from "@trigger.dev/sdk";
export const oneAtATime = task({ id: "one-at-a-time", queue: { concurrencyLimit: 1, }, run: async (payload) => { //... },});
```

# Updated @trigger.dev rules for v4-beta
created: 2025-04-23, last updated: 2026-03-22, [Read more](https://gist.github.com/hongkongkiwi/547201811128a7d23dcb4a269b5f4e1c)

| |---|
...
| | |
| |#### Queue options|
| | |
| |Control concurrency using predefined queues:|
| | |
| |```ts|
| |import { queue, task, logger } from "@trigger.dev/sdk";|
| | |
| |// Define a queue first|
| |const myQueue = queue({|
| |name: "my-queue",|
| |concurrencyLimit: 10,|
| |releaseConcurrencyOnWaitpoint: false, // New in v4|
| |});|
| | |
| |export const oneAtATime = task({|
| |id: "one-at-a-time",|
| |queue: myQueue, // Reference the queue|
| |run: async (payload, { ctx }) => {|
| |// Task logic|
| |},|
| |});|
| | |
| |// Or define queue inline|
| |export const anotherTask = task({|
| |id: "another-task",|
| |queue: {|
| |name: "another-queue",|
| |concurrencyLimit: 1,|
| |},|
| |run: async (payload, { ctx }) => {|
| |// Task logic|

# [TRI-5431] bug: [v4] queues get unpaused during deploy · Issue #2066 · triggerdotdev/trigger.dev
created: 2025-05-16, last updated: 2025-07-06, [Read more](https://github.com/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev/issues/2066)

In version 4 you can pause queues (from the dashboard or via the SDK).
Pausing sets a flag on `TaskQueue` in postgres and it also adjusts sets the concurrencyLimit of the queue to zero in Redis.
When deploys happen we aren't checking if a `TaskQueue` is paused, we're just setting the `concurrencyLimit` like normal from the deploy.
This unpauses the actual queue processing but leaves the `TaskQueue` status as paused.

# Queue Management | triggerdotdev/trigger.dev | DeepWiki
created: 2025-08-05, last updated: 2026-03-28, [Read more](https://deepwiki.com/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev/4.4-queue-management)

This document covers the queue management system in Trigger.dev, which handles the queuing, scheduling, and distribution of task executions across different runtime environments.
The system manages the flow from task triggering through execution completion, including message passing, concurrency control, and worker coordination.
...
The system implements multiple queue types optimized for different execution contexts and requirements.
...
### Queue Types and Properties
|Queue Type|Creation Context|Concurrency Control|Persistence|
|--|--|--|--|
|`NAMED`|Explicitly defined in task metadata|User-configurable limits|Persistent across deployments|
|`VIRTUAL`|Auto-generated per task|Task-level limits|Temporary, deployment-scoped|
...
The queue system integrates with multiple execution providers to handle different deployment scenarios and requirements.
...
## Concurrency and Resource Management
The queue system implements multiple layers of concurrency control to ensure efficient resource utilization and prevent system overload.
…
...
Task execution resources are controlled through machine presets that define CPU, memory, and cost parameters:

# Task Triggering and Scheduling | triggerdotdev/trigger.dev ...
created: 2025-12-13, last updated: 2026-03-28, [Read more](https://deepwiki.com/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev/3.2-task-triggering-and-scheduling)

|Parameter|Type|Description|
|--|--|--|
|`taskVersion`|string?|Task version|
|`concurrencyKey`|string?|Concurrency grouping|
|`queue`|object?|Queue configuration|
|`lockedQueueId`|string?|Specific queue ID|
|`isTest`|boolean|Test mode flag|
|`delayUntil`|Date?|Schedule for later|
|`maxAttempts`|number?|Override retry count|
|`ttl`|string?|Time-to-live for run|
|`tags`|Tag[]|Organizational tags|
|`parentTaskRunId`|string?|Parent run reference|
...
## Queue and Concurrency Configuration
### Queue Assignment
Tasks can be assigned to specific queues at trigger time:
…
The queue configuration determines:
- **Queue name**: Grouping for related tasks
- **Concurrency limit**: Max simultaneous runs for this queue
- **Priority**: Within-queue ordering (via `priorityMs`)
**Sources**: internal-packages/run-engine/src/engine/index.ts437-440
### Concurrency Keys
Concurrency keys enable grouping runs for concurrency control:
…
This ensures only one task runs at a time for each user, even if multiple are triggered.

# How Trigger.dev uses Redis: fair queues, checkpointing, and real ...
created: 2026-03-26, last updated: 2026-04-19, [Read more](https://indepth.dev/posts/2009/en/how-trigger-dev-uses-redis/)

# How Trigger.dev uses Redis: fair queues, checkpointing, and real-time streaming
Most people think of Redis as a cache.
Trigger.dev uses it as the backbone of a distributed task execution platform — for queuing, pub/sub, real-time streaming, concurrency control, and rate limiting.
All at once.
...
### Concurrency limiting with Lua
Trigger.dev supports multi-level concurrency limits: per-queue, per-tenant, per-organization, and global.
These are enforced atomically using Redis SETs and a Lua script:
```
-- Reserve concurrency across all groups, or reject entirely
for i = 1, numGroups do
local current = redis.call('SCARD', KEYS[i])
if current >= limits[i] then
return 0 -- At capacity, reject
end
end
-- All checks passed — add to all groups atomically
for i = 1, numGroups do
redis.call('SADD', KEYS[i], messageId)
end
return 1
```
…
= key limit hit,

# Quickstart - Trigger.dev - Mintlify
created: 2026-03-25, last updated: 2026-04-12, [Read more](https://mintlify.wiki/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev/quickstart)

You need Node.js 18.20.0 or later and an existing TypeScript or JavaScript project (Next.js, Remix, Express, etc.).
...
The CLI will:- Ask you to log in (or create an account) if you’re not already authenticated
- Ask which project to connect to
- Install `@trigger.dev/sdk` as a dependency
- Create a `trigger.config.ts` configuration file
- Create a `/trigger` directory with a sample `hello-world.ts` task
Your `trigger.config.ts` will look like this:
```
import { defineConfig } from "@trigger.dev/sdk";
export default defineConfig({
project: "<your-project-ref>",
dirs: ["./trigger"],
});
```
…
```
Trigger.dev v4.4.3
✔ Connected to Trigger.dev Cloud
✔ Watching /trigger for changes
Tasks:
• process-order trigger/order.ts
```
